questions.q: For the $7$-adic absolute value, which statement is correct?
questions.explain: $v_7(7)=1$, so $|7|_7 = 7^{-1} = 1/7$. Divisibility by $p$ makes a number $p$-adically small.

questions.q: Which inequality is the defining ultrametric property of $|\cdot|_p$?
questions.explain: Non-Archimedean absolute values satisfy the strong triangle inequality $|x+y|_p\le\max(|x|_p,|y|_p)$, which is strictly stronger than the usual triangle inequality.

questions.q: In an ultrametric space, which statement is true about any two closed balls $B_1, B_2$?
choices: They are either disjoint or one contains the other
Their union is always a ball
questions.explain: Because every point inside a ball is its center, two balls with a common point must be the same ball of radius $\max(r_1,r_2)$ — so any two balls are either disjoint or nested.

choices: $5$, by the ultrametric equality when norms differ
$\max(3,5)=5$ only as an upper bound; could be anything in $[0,5]$
Cannot be determined without more data
questions.explain: When $|x|_p \ne |y|_p$, the ultrametric inequality becomes an equality: $|x+y|_p = \max(|x|_p,|y|_p)$. This is the 'isoceles triangle' principle.

questions.q: How many connected components does $\mathbb{Q}_p$ have as a topological space? (Answer with the cardinality of singletons per point, i.e. $1$ per point means totally disconnected — here give the size of the connected component of any fixed point.)
questions.explain: $\mathbb{Q}_p$ is totally disconnected: the only connected subsets are singletons. So each point is its own connected component, of size $1$.
Using the geometric series identity $(p-1)\sum_{i\ge 0} p^i = -1$ in $|\cdot|_p$: with $p=5$, $4(1+5+5^2+\cdots) = -1$ in $\mathbb{Z}_5$.

questions.q: What is the first digit $a_0$ in the $3$-adic expansion of $7$ (i.e. $7 \bmod 3$)?
Two elements $x,y \in \mathbb{Z}_p$ satisfy $|x-y|_p = p^{-n}$ precisely when:
choices: their first $n$ digits agree and the $n$th disagrees
$x$ and $y$ are congruent mod $p^n$ but differ mod $p^{n+1}$
questions.explain: Digits agreeing through index $n-1$ is exactly the condition $x\equiv y\pmod{p^n}$, and disagreeing at index $n$ means $x\not\equiv y\pmod{p^{n+1}}$. Both descriptions are equivalent.

questions.q: Hensel's lemma (simple form) lifts $x_0$ with $f(x_0)\equiv 0\pmod p$ to a root in $\mathbb{Z}_p$ provided:
questions.explain: The derivative being a unit mod $p$ (i.e. nonzero mod $p$) is what makes Newton's iteration $x_{n+1}=x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$ well-defined and convergent in $\mathbb{Z}_p$.

questions.q: Seeding Hensel for $f(x) = x^2 - 2$ over $\mathbb{Z}_7$ with $x_0 = 3$, the next iterate $x_1 = x_0 + a_1\cdot 7$ uses digit $a_1 = -(x_0^2-2)/7 \cdot (2x_0)^{-1} \bmod 7$. Compute $a_1$.
questions.explain: $(3^2-2)/7 = 1$. Also $2\cdot 3 = 6$, and $6^{-1} \equiv 6 \pmod 7$. So $a_1 \equiv -1\cdot 6 \equiv -6 \equiv 1 \pmod 7$. Hence $x_1 = 3 + 1\cdot 7 = 10$, and indeed $10^2 = 100 \equiv 2 \pmod{49}$.

choices: Yes, because $2^2 = 4 \equiv -1 \pmod 5$ and $2\cdot 2 = 4 \not\equiv 0 \pmod 5$, so Hensel lifts
questions.explain: Take $f(x)=x^2+1$. Then $f(2)=5\equiv 0\pmod 5$ and $f'(2)=4\not\equiv 0\pmod 5$. Hensel produces a unique $i\in\mathbb{Z}_5$ with $i\equiv 2\pmod 5$.

questions.q: The Hasse-Minkowski theorem says that a quadratic form $Q$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ represents zero nontrivially over $\mathbb{Q}$ iff:
choices: it does so over $\mathbb{R}$ and over every $\mathbb{Q}_p$
its discriminant is a perfect square
questions.explain: Hasse-Minkowski: for quadratic forms, local solvability at all places (the real place plus every $p$-adic place) is equivalent to global solvability over $\mathbb{Q}$.

questions.q: Consider $x^2 + y^2 = 3$ over $\mathbb{Q}$. Using Hasse-Minkowski, which obstruction rules out rational solutions?
choices: Real obstruction: the left side is non-negative so cannot equal $3$
Local obstruction at $p=3$: the equation has no primitive solutions mod $9$
There is no obstruction; rational solutions exist
Local obstruction at $p=2$: $x,y$ must both be even, forcing a contradiction
questions.explain: $x^2+y^2=3$ is clearly solvable in $\mathbb{R}$. The $3$-adic obstruction: mod $3$, squares are $\{0,1\}$, so $x^2+y^2\in\{0,1,2\}\bmod 3$, never $3\equiv 0$ with $(x,y)$ primitive. This local failure forces no rational solution.

questions.q: The local-global principle FAILS for some cubic forms. What kind of object typically measures this failure for elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$?
questions.explain: $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ parametrises locally trivial principal homogeneous spaces for $E$ — objects that have points in every $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and in $\mathbb{R}$ but not in $\mathbb{Q}$. It is the standard measure of local-global failure for elliptic curves.
